---
title: "Python Tip: `any()` and `all()`"
subtitle: "Check multiple conditions with two built-in functions"
author: "Shep Bryan IV"
description: "Learn how to use any and all to check multiple Boolean values and conditions in Python."
---



<!-- PANEL 1 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

:::{.center}

# Python Tip: `any()` and `all()`

Need to know if **at least one** condition is true?  
Want to check if **every** condition is true?  
Use `any()` and `all()` for clean, readable checks!  

:::{.python-logo}
:::

:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 2 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Using `any()`

`any()` returns `True` if **at least one** element is true.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
checks = [False, True, False]

# Don't do this
found = False
for check in checks:
    if check:
        found = True
print(found)

# Do this instead
print(any(checks))
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 3 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Using `all()`

`all()` returns `True` only if **every** item is true.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
checks = [True, True, True]

print(all(checks))

checks = [True, False, True]

print(all(checks))
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 4 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Checking Conditions

Wrap them around a condition to see if **any** or **all** elements meet it.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
scores = [82, 95, 67, 88]

# Did anyone score 90 or higher?
print(any(score >= 90 for score in scores))

# Did everyone pass?
print(all(score >= 60 for score in scores))
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 5 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Empty Collections

With no items, `any()` is `False`, while `all()` is `True`.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
items = []

print(any(items))
print(all(items))

# There is no true item for any() to find.
# There is no false item for all() to find.
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 6 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

:::{.center}

## Wrap-Up

Use `any()` when **at least one** condition must be true and `all()` when **every** condition must be true!

Follow me for more tips.  
Shep Bryan IV

:::

:::
